trajectory_summary: The call moves through verify checkboxes at a functional pace but never builds energy. Joel captures the bill figure and prior-inquiry history — both potential leverage points — and walks past both without anchoring or diagnosing. The call ends with a soft callback promise, no locked time, no button-up protocol, and the title question unresolved. The structural gaps (no appointment, no button-up, no spouse/title confirmation, no bill collected) mean this is a lead in limbo, not a booked sit.
outcome_dispute: If v1 tagged this call as 'booked,' that tag is incorrect. The call ends with Joel offering a callback 'tomorrow in the morning' with no confirmed time, no appointment structure, no button-up elements, and no bill collected. This is a soft callback, not a booked appointment. No-show risk is high. The schema distinction between 'callbacks WITH locked times vs we'll touch base' (analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction) applies directly here.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill anchor flip — converting captured pain into emotional leverage
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Dang. Okay. Yeah. Those are up there for sure.
primary_coaching_focus.why: John handed Joel a $300/month Dominion bill and Joel filed it as a data point. The bill flip is the highest-leverage available move on this call: it converts John's passive curiosity posture into a financial reason to stay engaged. The schema marks this as the universal floor coaching gap — zero flip instances across the corpus. This is the right call to introduce the mechanic because the bill figure is clear, the pain is real, and the missed opportunity is unambiguous.

key_moments.quote: No. I I I wasn't interested in that. So I already had the the the the the discount, and then that's something that's trying to find out how much was it and everything.
key_moments.stewart_read: John's opening reframe is the most important moment in the call. He's not a motivated solar buyer — he's a passive incentive-browser. The schema marks interest_in_solar as 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' and specifies that 'depth matters more than the answer' and 'top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' Joel spends under 20 seconds, mirrors the low-investment framing, and moves on. The strategic read — that Joel was deliberately accepting the low frame to build trust before re-engaging — is not supported; there is no later re-engagement attempt. This is a reactive miss on the call's highest-leverage verify item.

key_moments.quote: Dang. Okay. Yeah. Those are up there for sure.
key_moments.stewart_read: John has just disclosed a ~$300/month Dominion bill. This is the single strongest emotional anchor in the call. The schema's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip' — Joel executes 'ask + acknowledge + move on.' The flip would have been: 'So you're paying Dominion $300 a month right now — that's the expense solar replaces, not adds to.' Instead, Joel says 'dang' and pivots to prior designs. The bill pain is real; the leverage is abandoned.

Joel asks 'Is there anyone else on the title of the home with you at all?' and John's answer — 'It's been a while' — is ambiguous. It could mean he's been the sole owner for a while, or it could mean he hasn't thought about it in a while, or it could signal a life change (divorce, spousal loss). Joel responds with 'Okay. Sounds good.' and moves on without clarifying. The schema marks title/co-owner confirmation as required and critical for spouse coordination at button-up. This is an unresolved qualification item that could surface as a deal-killer at the closer stage.

key_moments.quote: I about six, eight months ago.
key_moments.stewart_read: John discloses he looked into solar six to eight months ago. This is the highest-diagnostic moment in the call: something stopped him from moving forward. Joel's response is a generic equipment-improvement comment ('the equipment has gotten a lot better') rather than the obvious diagnostic question: 'What stopped you from moving forward at that point?' The answer to that question would have surfaced the real barrier — price, spouse, credit, skepticism — and given Joel something concrete to address or route to the closer.
